rows.name: five-year note
rows.desc: A five-year note is a type of U.S. Treasury security with a fixed interest rate and a maturity date five years from issuance, issued by the U.S. Department of the Treasury to finance government spending. It is an intermediate-term debt instrument that pays interest semiannually and is considered a benchmark for pricing other fixed-income securities. Five-year notes are widely used by investors seeking a balance between yield and duration risk in a low-credit-risk environment.

judge.note: Accurate, well-grounded description of a standard financial instrument.

rows.name: five-year note
rows.desc: A five-year note is a U.S. Treasury security with a fixed interest rate and a maturity of exactly five years from the date of issuance. It is sold at auction to investors and pays interest semiannually until maturity, at which point the face value is repaid. These notes are widely used by investors seeking a moderate-term, low-risk investment backed by the full faith and credit of the U.S. government.

judge.note: Accurate, well-grounded description of a standard financial instrument.

It operates as a plaintiff-side firm with numerous offices across the United States and handles a high volume of cases involving consumer protection, medical malpractice, and product liability. The firm is consistently recognized as one of the largest plaintiff law firms in the U.S. by number of attorneys and case filings.

judge.note: Well-grounded, no fabricated claims.
